content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 25, 2017

Joshua Nelson
Best Best & Krieger, LLP
500 Capitol Mall, Suite 1700
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-186

Dear Mr. Nelson:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the North Tahoe Public Utility
District (the "District") regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act") and Section 1090 et seq. Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of
interest provisions of the Act and Section 1090, and we offer no opinion on the applicability of
other conflict of interest laws, such as the Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of
interest. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops.
71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Placer County
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a
written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that,
for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding
against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTIONS

1. Does the Act prohibit the President of the District's Board of Directors, Lane Lewis, from
taking part in District decisions relating to the potential separation of the District's and Woodvista
Golf Course's respective rights to divert water from Lake Tahoe under State Water Resources
Control Board (SWRCB) Permit P-13525?

2. Does Section 1090 prohibit the President from making or participating in, or the District
from entering into, an arrangement with Woodvista Golf Course to jointly submit the application to
separate their respective diversion rights and to share the joint application's costs?

___________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-186
Page No. 2

CONCLUSIONS

1. The Act prohibits the President from taking part in District decisions relating to the
potential separation of the District's and Woodvista Golf Course's respective diversion rights
because those decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the
President's financial interest in the Golf Course.

2. No. Section 1090 does not prohibit the President from making or participating in, or the
District from entering into, an arrangement with Woodvista Golf Course to jointly submit the
application to separate their respective diversion rights and to share the joint applicant's costs
because the modification of a regulatory permit does not involve a "contract" for purposes of
Section 1090. However, because the rule of necessity may also apply, the President should abstain
from making or participating in the arrangement out of an abundance of caution.

FACTS

Your firm acts as the General Counsel for the District, and the District authorized your firm
to pursue this advice on its behalf.

Lane Lewis is the President of the District's Board of Directors. The President has served on
the Board for approximately twenty-seven years. Prior to the President's first term on the Board, he
purchased a golf course located within the District, the Old Brookway Golf Course. The Golf
Course was previously owned by the President's father and then by Oakwood Investment
Company. The President personally owns the real property underlying the Golf Course.

The Golf Course is operated by Woodvista Golf Course, a California corporation. The
President receives rental income of over $100,000 per year from Woodvista Golf Course for use of
the real property underlying the Golf Course. The President also receives rental income through the
Golf Course from a tenant using a portion of that real property as a restaurant.

In 1967, the District purchased the Brockway Water Company from Oakwood Investment
Company. At the time of this purchase, Oakwood Investment Company owned both the Brockway
Water Company and the Golf Course. The District's purchase of the Brockway Water Company
included the Brockway Water Company's right to divert water from Lake Tahoe, but expressly
excluded the Golf Course's right to divert water from Lake Tahoe. The Golf Course has continued
to hold and use its diversion right since the District's purchase of the Brockway Water Company.

One of the three water rights the District acquired from the Brockway Water Company is set
forth in SWRCB Permit 13525. Because the purchase of the Brockway Water Company expressly
excluded the Golf Course's diversion right, Permit 13525 memorializes both the District's and the
Golf Course's respective diversion rights. The SWRCB has recognized this unique situation in
which two separate rights are memorialized in a single permit multiple times, and has repeatedly
recommended that the District and the Golf Course separate their respective diversion rights
memorialized in the Permit so that each party will be able to conclusively determine its rights and
responsibilities with respect to its particular diversion right.

File No. A-17-186
Page No. 3

The respective diversion rights set forth in Permit 13525, however, have not been separated
to date. Rather, the District has continued to operate and maintain the prior Brockway Water
Company water system, which does not utilize the intake facility used by the Golf Course. The Golf
Course separately operates and maintains its intake facility and all related irrigation facilities.

Senate Bill 88 ("SB 88") was adopted in 2015. Among other things, this enacted legislation
requires certain water diverters to accurately measure and report their water diversions. The
SWRCB has adopted regulations to implement this requirement. Pursuant to those regulations,
beginning July 1, 2017, a diverter with the right to divert 100 acre-feet of water per year or more,
which includes the District, must install measuring devices on all its diversions, certify that it has
installed these devices, and report diversion data on its diversions. The SWRCB may extend this
deadline. Going forward, accurate diversion data must be maintained for ten years and reported to
the SWRCB annually and as otherwise requested.

Because the District is the primary holder of all the diversion rights memorialized in Permit
15325, SB 88 will require the District to install measuring devices on all diversions authorized in
the Permit, including the Golf Course's distinct separate intake facility for its diversion, certify
those devices, and report data for those diversions.

ANALYSIS

The Act

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in the
making of, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a
"financial interest" in a decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will have material financial effect on one or more of the official's interests identified in
that section. Of those interests, the facts presented and the decisions relating to potential separation
of the District and the Golf Course's respective rights to divert water from Lake Tahoe implicate
the following interests of the President:

• Any business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment worth $2,000 or
more (Section 87103(a)), or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d)). The President purchased
the Golf Course prior to joining the Board. The Golf Course is operated by Woodvista Golf
Course, a California corporation, and the President receives more than $100,000 in rental
income per year from Woodvista Golf Course. Assuming the President has an investment worth
$2,000 or more in, or holds a management position with, Woodvista Golf Course, he would
have an interest in that business entity.

• Any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more.
(Section 87103(b).) The President personally owns the real property underlying the Golf
Course. So long as that interest is worth $2,000 or more, the President would have an interest in
that real property.

File No. A-17-186
Page No. 4

• Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the
regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status,
aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the official within 12
months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) Because the President receives rental income
greater than $100,000 per year from Woodvista Golf Course, the President has an interest in the
Golf Course as a source of income.²

• An interest in his or her personal finances and those of immediate family members. (See Section
87103.) An official always has an interest in his or her personal finances.

Therefore, the President has interests in Woodvista Golf Course as a business entity and as a
source of income, in his real property underlying the Golf Course, and in his personal finances.

Foreseeability and Materiality

Regulation 18701(a) provides that a financial effect on an official's financial interest is
presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a named party in or the subject of a
governmental decision before the official's agency.

Because Woodvista Golf Course is a named party in, or the subject of, the decisions relating
to the potential separation of the District and the Golf Course's respective rights to divert water
from Lake Tahoe, the financial effect on the President's interests in the Golf Course will be
presumed to be reasonably foreseeable.

Regulation 18702.1(a)(7) provides that a decision's reasonably foreseeable financial effect
on an official's business interest is material whenever the business entity is "subject to an action
taken by the official's agency, the effect of which is directed solely at the business entity in which
the official has an interest."

The decisions at issue will determine whether the District will pursue separation of the
District's and the Golf Course's respective water diversion rights memorialized in Permit 13525.
These decisions are not directed at any other business entity other than the Golf Course. Therefore,
the Golf Course is subject to an action by the District directed solely at Woodvista Golf Course.

Thus, the Act prohibits the President from taking part in District decisions relating to the
potential separation of the District's and the Golf Course's respective rights to divert water from
Lake Tahoe because those decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on Woodvista Golf Course.³

² Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the materiality standards applicable to a decision's reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on an official's interest in a source of income. However, if the source of income is a business entity,
Regulation 18702.3(a)(4) provides that materiality is determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1, which sets forth the
materiality standards applicable to a decision's effect on an official's interest in a business entity. Therefore, the
materiality of the effect of the decisions at issue on the official's interest in Woodvista Golf Course, whether analyzed
as a business interest or a source-of-income interest, would be determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1.

³ Because we have already determined that the Act prohibits the President from taking part in the decisions at
issue, we do not further analyze his other financial interests implicated by those decisions.

File No. A-17-186
Page No. 5

Section 1090

Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has
a financial interest."¹ A contract that violates Section 1090 is void, and the prohibition applies
regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties.²

For Section 1090 to apply, the matter at issue must involve a contract. Traditional contract
principles are applied to determine whether a contract is involved for purposes of Section 1090.³
However, both the Attorney General's Office and the Commission have previously opined that a
modification of a regulatory permit is a regulatory matter that does not involve a "contract" for
purposes of Section 1090.⁴

In that Attorney General Opinion, the owner of an ambulance service that held a certificate
of public convenience and necessity from the city authorizing the ambulance service to operate
within the city was elected the mayor of the city. The Attorney General determined that Section
1090 did not prohibit the owner of the ambulance service operating under the certificate from
holding the office of mayor of the city.⁵ The Attorney General also determined that the city's
modification of a rate schedule that was incorporated as a term of the certificate was a regulatory
matter and not a "contract" for purposes of Section 1090.

Similar to the modification of the certificate in that Attorney General Opinion, the potential
arrangement between the District and the Woodvista Golf Course to jointly apply to the SWRCB to
modify Permit I3525 and to share the application's costs is also a regulatory matter. Therefore,
Section 1090 does not prohibit the President from making or participating in, or the District from
entering into, an arrangement with the Golf Course to jointly submit the application to separate their
respective diversion rights and to share the application's costs because the modification of a
regulatory permit is a regulatory matter that does not involve a "contract" for purposes of Section
1090.⁶

Even assuming we were to find the potential arrangement between the District and the Golf
Course to be a contract under Section 1090, which we do not, the "rule of necessity" exception
would nonetheless apply to allow the District and the Golf Course to enter into the arrangement.
Previously, the California Supreme Court has applied the rule of necessity in a non-procurement
situation to a city council where members of the council had a conflict of interest that otherwise

⁴ People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.

⁵ Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646-649.

⁶ 89 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 258, 260 (2006); 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36 (2001); 78 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 230, 234
(1995).

⁷ 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34 (2001); Carvalho Advice Letter, No. A-16-265; Miller Advice Letter, No. A-15-
229.

⁸ 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36 (2001).

⁹ Id. at pp. 36-37.

File No. A-17-186
Page No. 6

would have prevented action by the city council.10 When applied in non-procurement situations, the
rule of necessity ensures that essential government functions are performed even where a conflict of
interest exists.11

Based on the facts presented, SB 88 and the SWRCB's regulations implementing it require
certain water diverters, including the District with respect to its diversion right memorialized in
Permit 13525, to install measuring devices on all diversion points, certify it has done so, and report
accurate diversion data to the SWRCB. If the District and Woodvista Golf Course do not separate
their respective diversion rights under Permit 13525, SB 88 and its implementing regulations will
require the District to install and certify a measuring device on the Golf Course's separate diversion
point and to report diversion data from that device to SWRCB. Because compliance with SB 88 and
its implementing regulations with respect to the Golf Course's separate diversion would
significantly increase the cost of the District's overall compliance with those provisions, potentially
putting the District's comprehensive budget in jeopardy, it is an essential duty of the District to
modify Permit 13525 to separate the District's and the Golf Course's respective diversion rights.
Therefore, even if the potential arrangement between the District and the Golf Course is a
"contract" for purposes of Section 1090, the rule of necessity would allow the District to enter into
that arrangement.

Where the rule of necessity applies to allow a multi-member body to act when it otherwise
would have been precluded from doing so due to one or more members' conflict of interest, the
member or members with the conflict of interest should abstain from participation.12 Therefore, out
of an abundance of caution, the President should abstain from making or participating in the making
of the potential arrangement between the District and the Golf Course.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Jack C. Woodside
General Counsel

By:     Matthew F. Christy
        Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl

__________________________

10 Federal Construction Co. v. Curd, 179 Cal. 489, 493 (1918).

11 69 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 102, 109 (1986); 65 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 305, 310 (1982).

12 89 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 217, 222 (2006); 88 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 106, 112 (2005); 69 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 102,
111 (1986).

sections.question:
I. Does the Act prohibit the President of the District's Board of Directors, Lane Lewis, from 
taking part in District decisions relating to the potential separation of the District's and Woodvista 
Golf Course's respective rights to divert water from Lake Tahoe under State Water Resources 
Control Board (SWRCB) Pennit P-13525? 
2. Does Section I 090 prohibit the President from making or participating in, or the District 
from entering into, an arrangement with Woodvista Golf Course to jointly submit the application to 
separate their respective diversion rights and to share the joint application's costs?
sections.conclusion:
I. The Act prohibits the President from taking part in District decisions relating to the 
potential separation of the District's and Woodvista Golf Course's respective diversion rights 
because those decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the 
President's financial interest in the Golf Course. 
2. No. Section 1090 <locs not prohibit the President from making ur participating in, ur the 
District from entering into, an arrangement with Woodvista Golf Course to jointly submit the 
application to separate their respective diversion rights and to share the joint application's costs 
because the modification of a regulatory permit does not involve a "contract" for purposes of 
Section 1090. However, because the rule of necessity may also apply, the President should abstain 
from making or participating in the arrangement out of an abundance of caution.

sections.analysis:
The Act 
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in the 
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in 
which the official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a 
"financial interest" in a decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that 
the decision will have material financial effect on one or more of the official's interests identified in 
that section. Of those interests, the facts presented and the decisions relating to potential separation 
of the District and the Golf Course's respective rights to divert water from Lake Tahoe implicate 
the following interests of the President: 
• 
Any business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment worth $2,000 or 
more (Section 87 I 03(a)), or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103( d)). The President purchased 
the Golf Course prior to joining the Board, the Golf Course is operated by Woodvista Golf 
Course, a California corporation, and the President receives more than $ I 00,000 in rental 
income per year from Woodvista Golf Course. Assuming the President has an investment worth 
$2,000 or more in, or holds a management position with, Woodvista Golf Course, he would 
have an interest in that business entity. 
• Any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more. 
(Section 87103(b).) The President personally owns the real property underlying the Golf 
Course. So long as that interest is worth $2,000 or more, the President would have an interest in 
that real property.  
• Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the 
regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, 
aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the official within 12 
months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) Because the President receives rental income 
greater than$ 100,000 per year from Woodvista Golf Course, the President has an interest in the 
Golf Course as a source of income.2 
• An interest in his or her personal finances and those of immediate family members. (See Section 
87103.) An official always has an interest in his or her personal finances. 
Therefore, the President has interests in Woodvista Golf Course as a business entity and as a 
source of income, in his real property underlying the Golf Course, and in his personal finances. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Regulation 18701(a) provides that a financial effect on an official's financial interest is 
presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a named party in or the subject of a 
governmental decision before the official's agency. 
Because Woodvista Golf Course is a named party in, or the subject of, the decisions relating 
to the potential separation of the District and the Golf Course's respective rights to divert water 
from Lake Tahoe, the financial effect on the President's interests in the Golf Course will be 
presumed to be reasonably foreseeable. 
Regulation 18702.l(a)(?) provides that a decision's reasonably foreseeable financial effect 
on an official's business interest is material whenever the business entity is "subject to an action 
taken by the official's agency, the effect of which is directed solely at the business entity in which 
the official has an interest." 
The decisions at issue will determine whether the District will pursue separation of the 
District's and the Golf Course's respective water diversion rights memorialized in Permit 13525. 
These decisions are not directed at any other business entity other than the Golf Course. Therefore, 
the Golf Course is subject to an action by the District directed solely at Woodvista Golf Course. 
Thus, the Act prohibits the President from taking part in District decisions relating to the 
potential separation of the District's and the Golf Course's respective rights to divert water from 
Lake Tahoe because those decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect 
on Wood vista Golf Course.3 
2 Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the materiality standards applicable to a decision's reasonably foreseeable 
financial effect on an official's interest in a source of income. However, if the source of income is a business entity, 
Regulation l 8702.3(a)(4) provides that materiality is determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1, which sets forth the 
materiality standards applicable to a decision's effect on an official's interest in a business entity. Therefore, the 
materiality of the effect of the decisions at issue on the official's interest in Woodvista Golf Course, whether analyzed 
as a business interest or a source-of-income interest, would be determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1. 
3 Because we have already determined that the Act prohibits the President from taking part in the decisions at 
issue, we do not further analyze his other financial interests implicated by those decisions.  
Section 1090 
Under Section I 090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has 
a financial interest. "4 A contract that violates Section l 090 is void, and the prohibition applies 
regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties.5 
For Section 1090 to apply, the matter at issue must involve a contract. Traditional contract

of public convenience and necessity from the city authorizing the ambulance service to operate 
within the city was elected the mayor of the city. The Attorney General determined that Section 
I 090 did not prohibit the owner of the ambulance service operating under the certificate from 
holding the office of mayor of the city.8 The Attorney General also determined that the city's

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: I. Does the Act prohibit the President of the District's Board of Directors, Lane Lewis, from 
taking part in District decisions relating to the potential separation of the District's and Woodvista 
Golf Course's respective rights to divert water from Lake Tahoe under State Water Resources 
Control Board (SWRCB) Pennit P-13525? 
2. Does Section I 090 prohibit the President from making or participating in, or the District 
from entering into, an arrangement with Woodvista Golf Course to jointly submit the application to 
separate their respective diversion rights and to share the joint application's costs?

CONCLUSION: I. The Act prohibits the President from taking part in District decisions relating to the 
potential separation of the District's and Woodvista Golf Course's respective diversion rights 
because those decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the 
President's financial interest in the Golf Course. 
2. No. Section 1090 <locs not prohibit the President from making ur participating in, ur the 
District from entering into, an arrangement with Woodvista Golf Course to jointly submit the 
application to separate their respective diversion rights and to share the joint application's costs 
because the modification of a regulatory permit does not involve a "contract" for purposes of 
Section 1090. However, because the rule of necessity may also apply, the President should abstain 
from making or participating in the arrangement out of an abundance of caution.

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and Section 1090, and we offer no opinion on the applicability of other conflict of interest laws, such as the Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of interest. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Placer County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).) QUESTIONS 1. Does the Act prohibit the President of the District's Board of Directors, Lane Lewis, from taking part in District decisions relating to the potential separation of the District's and Woodvista Golf Course's respective rights to divert water from Lake Tahoe under State Water Resources Control Board (SWRCB) Permit P-13525? 2. Does Section 1090 prohibit the President from making or participating in, or the District from entering into, an arrangement with Woodvista Golf Course to jointly submit the application to separate their respective diversion rights and to share the joint application's costs? ___________________________ ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-17-186 Page No. 2 CONCLUSIONS 1. The Act prohibits the President from taking part in District decisions relating to the potential separation of the District's and Woodvista Golf Course's respective diversion rights because those decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the President's financial interest in the Golf Course. 2.
